title: The Steppe Inheritance
bullets: Before the Türk revolt, the Mongolian steppe passed through a sequence of nomadic powers — Xiongnu, Xianbei, Rouran — each refining the political template of supreme khan, eastern and western wings, decimal military organization, and annual tribal assembly.

The Rouran Khaganate ruled from roughly 402 to 552 and were the first steppe rulers to take the title khagan, the great-khan title that every later Inner Asian empire from the Türks to Genghis Khan would inherit.

The Ashina clan — meaning Noble Wolf in proto-Turkic — were Rouran ironworking vassals in the southern Altai Mountains, mining iron and forging the lance heads, arrowheads, and stirrups for their overlords' cavalry.

The Türks were the first steppe people to make iron stirrups standard issue, multiplying the combat effectiveness of mounted archers and lance-fighters; the technology spread westward via Türk contact with Byzantium and transformed medieval European warfare.

The Türk founding myth has the Ashina descended from a small boy — sole survivor of a tribal massacre — nursed by a she-wolf in a mountain cave and fathering ten sons by her, the founders of the ten Türk clans.

Bumin built up his political following inside the Rouran khaganate through the 540s, married a Western Wei princess in 551, then attacked and destroyed the Rouran field army in 552, sending the last Rouran khagan Anagui to suicide.

The surviving Rouran fled west and may have been the same people who appeared a few years later on the Hungarian plain as the Avars, the new steppe terror of the Byzantine Empire under Justinian and his successors.

Bumin took the imperial title qaghan and proclaimed himself the founder of the Türk Khaganate — the first political leader in history known to have called himself and his people Türk, the ethnonym that would eventually be carried by every Turkic-speaker from Istanbul to Xinjiang.
subtitle: an ironworking vassal clan in the Altai Mountains revolts against the Rouran

title: Bumin Qaghan and the Founding
bullets: Within thirty years of Bumin's 552 revolt, the Türk Khaganate stretched 4,500 miles from Manchuria to the Caspian Sea — the largest political unit yet seen on Earth, larger than the Roman and Sasanian empires combined at their peaks.

The geographic heart of the new empire was the Orkhon Valley in central Mongolia, where every later steppe empire (Uyghur, Mongol) would establish its capital — Genghis Khan's Karakorum was built on almost the same site seven centuries later.

The Türk political system used a dual kingship: a senior khagan in the eastern Mongolian heartland and a junior yabghu in the western wing, with the western yabghu given near-equal authority — a structural design flaw that would split the empire in 582.

Bumin died within months of his triumph and was succeeded by his son Muqan Qaghan, who consolidated the eastern half of the empire across two decades, conquering the Khitan in Manchuria and the Kyrgyz in southern Siberia.

Bumin's brother Istemi held the title yabghu of the western wing and pushed the Türk frontier all the way to the Volga, allying with Sasanian Persia, opening contact with Byzantine Constantinople, and effectively founding the Western Türk Khaganate.

Muqan extracted 100,000 bolts of silk per year from each of the divided Chinese north's two dynasties — Northern Zhou and Northern Qi — by playing them against each other, the largest tribute payment China had made to the steppe since the Han heqin treaties.

The Türk-Sogdian symbiosis was the structural innovation that made the empire possible: the Türks provided military protection and overland security, the Sogdians provided commercial expertise, language skills, diplomatic experience, and a working alphabet.

By 575 the Türks had built the largest political unit in history up to that point, in roughly twenty-five years from a standing start, by combining steppe military power with Sogdian commercial sophistication.
subtitle: two brothers, the Orkhon heartland, and the largest empire yet built

title: The Sogdian Connection
bullets: The Sogdians were an Iranian-speaking people from the oases of Bukhara and Samarkand in modern Uzbekistan, speaking a Middle Iranian language as different from Persian as Spanish is from French — and operating the merchant network that ran the Silk Road on the khaganate's behalf.

Türk khagans worked across three completely unrelated language families: Old Turkic with their warriors, Sogdian with their merchants, and Chinese through interpreters with envoys from Chang'an — Sogdian was the lingua franca of the Silk Road for centuries.

Sogdian merchant colonies stretched from Constantinople to Chang'an, with branch offices at Dunhuang, Turfan, Kucha, Khotan, Kashgar, Suyab, and the lower Volga — a true merchant diaspora comparable to the later Sephardic Jewish and Armenian networks.

The Sogdian solution to long-distance trade risk was the family network: a Bukhara merchant sent silk via a relative at Dunhuang to another relative at Chang'an, with kinship serving as a credit, information, and insurance system simultaneously.

Sogdian script, descended from the Aramaic alphabet, became the Türks' first written language; only in the early eighth century did the Türks develop a custom script — the Old Turkic runiform script of the Orkhon Inscriptions.

The Türk-Sogdian arrangement aligned the khaganate's interests with the Silk Road's interests, giving the empire every reason to keep trade routes open and to fight wars only when they served commercial as well as political ends.

Sasanian Shah Khusrau I refused to allow Sogdian silk into Persian markets, then poisoned a Türk embassy that came to negotiate — provoking the Türk decision to bypass Persia and send Maniakh's embassy directly to Constantinople in 568.

The Sogdian merchant diaspora is one of the largest and longest-lasting commercial networks in pre-modern history, achieving a level of integration with its patron empire that few other merchant diasporas have matched.
subtitle: the merchant network that ran the Silk Road on the khaganate's behalf

title: Diplomatic Acrobatics
bullets: The Türks crushed the Hephthalite Empire — the White Huns who had killed Sasanian Shah Peroz I in 484 — by allying with Khusrau I in 557 and attacking simultaneously from north and south, with the Oxus River set as the new boundary.

The Türk-Sasanian alliance against the Hephthalites is a textbook example of how the steppe and the settled world could combine to crush a rival neither could destroy alone — the same pattern would recur with Han China against the Xiongnu and the Mongols against Khwarezm.

When Khusrau closed Persian markets to Sogdian silk and poisoned a Türk embassy, Istemi sent the Sogdian merchant Maniakh on an unprecedented mission: bypass Persia entirely, travel north of the Caspian, and negotiate directly with Byzantine Constantinople.

Justin II received Maniakh in Constantinople in 568 and sent the Byzantine ambassador Zemarchus back to Istemi's court the following year — opening the first sustained diplomatic contact between East Rome and the eastern steppe.

Zemarchus's account of the Türk court, preserved by the Byzantine historian Menander Protector, is the earliest extensive Western description of the khaganate in operation — including the Türk shamans waving smoking branches of incense over the Byzantine envoys.

Istemi received Zemarchus in a vast circular felt tent decorated with multicolored silks, sat on a wheeled gold throne, and made the Byzantines drink prodigious quantities of kumis — fermented mare's milk, the universal alcoholic drink of the steppe.

The Maniakh-Zemarchus exchange of 568-569 represents one of the earliest direct East-West diplomatic contacts in recorded history, with Sogdian merchants providing the language skills and commercial infrastructure to make trans-Eurasian diplomacy possible.

For about twenty years in the late sixth century, the great-power chessboard of Eurasia genuinely included a steppe nomadic empire as a top-tier player, with simultaneous Türk relations stretching from Constantinople to Chang'an.
subtitle: Maniakh's embassy to Byzantium, Zemarchus at the Türk court, and the first East-West diplomatic axis

title: The East-West Split
bullets: After Taspar Qaghan's death in 581, three branches of the Ashina dynasty disputed the succession; by 582 the empire had split formally into Eastern and Western Türk Khaganates, with the Altai Mountains as the rough boundary.

Tardu Yabghu, Istemi's son, ruled the Western Türks for nearly thirty years and tried in 599 to reunite both halves under his sole rule, briefly proclaiming himself sole great khagan before his subject peoples revolted and his bid collapsed in 603.

The Sui Dynasty reunified China in 589, stopped the silk tribute that had funded the Türk treasury, and supported every Türk faction against every other Türk faction — fueling the civil wars that prevented Türk unity from being restored.

Eastern Türk khagan Shibi besieged the Sui Emperor Yang Guang at Yanmen Pass in 615 — a deliberate replay of the Xiongnu humiliation of Han Gaozu at Baideng eight centuries earlier — hastening the Sui collapse and the rise of the Tang.

Tang founder Li Yuan started his rebellion against the Sui in 617 by paying tribute to Shibi and accepting nominal Türk overlordship in exchange for cavalry support — a humiliating debt the Tang would repay with a hammer thirteen years later.

In the winter of 629-630, the Tang general Li Jing led a 100,000-strong winter campaign across the frozen Gobi Desert and surprised Illig Qaghan in his Yinshan camp; the Eastern Türk Khaganate ceased to exist as an independent state within weeks.

Tang Taizong was proclaimed Tian Kehan — Heavenly Kaghan — by the surrendered Türk leaders, becoming the only Chinese emperor in history to formally hold a steppe imperial title alongside the Mandate of Heaven.

The Tang general Su Dingfang destroyed the Western Türk Khaganate in 657-659, capturing the last Western khagan Ashina Helu at the Battle of the Irtysh River and bringing all of Central Asia under Tang protectorate rule for the next half century.
subtitle: civil war fractures the empire and Tang Taizong takes both halves

title: Under the Tang
bullets: Tang Taizong sided with the advisor Wen Yanbo against Wei Zheng's relocation proposal, leaving the conquered Türks in the Ordos region under Tang prefectural supervision but allowed to keep their tribal organization, their pastoral economy, and their Türk identity.

The Tang Protectorate System worked because it gave the Türks something to lose: cooperation brought salaries, titles, and security; rebellion brought the full weight of the most effective army in Eurasia.

After Su Dingfang's 657-659 conquest of the Western Türks, the Tang created the Anxi Protectorate in the Tarim Basin to govern the Silk Road, with the Four Garrisons of Anxi at Kucha, Khotan, Kashgar, and Karashahr or Suyab.

The parallel Beiting Protectorate in Dzungaria handled the steppe peoples of the western frontier, and together Anxi and Beiting governed a vast territory that had been the heart of an independent Türk khaganate less than thirty years earlier.

The Sogdians continued to operate the commercial network they had run under the Türks, and Tang protectorate rule was actually good for Sogdian merchants: more reliable security, more predictable taxation, and access to Chang'an, the most cosmopolitan city in the world.

By 670 things were going wrong: the Tibetan Empire was pushing into the Tarim Basin from the south, Empress Wu Zetian's rise was destabilizing the Tang court, and a new generation of Türk warriors had grown up under Tang rule with no personal investment in cooperation.

Ashide Wenfu's revolt in 679 was suppressed within a year but demonstrated that Türk political identity had survived fifty years of Tang rule — and that the Tang policy of preserving tribal organization had preserved exactly the social structure that could organize a revolt.

In 682 Kutlugh — a junior Ashina noble serving as a Tang frontier officer — led a small revolt that would re-establish the Türk Khaganate within three years and end the Tang protectorate over the Mongolian heartland forever.
subtitle: fifty years of Tang protectorate rule that almost worked

title: The Second Khaganate and Bilge Kagan
bullets: Kutlugh — taking the throne name Ilterish Qaghan, the One Who Gathered the People — led the 682 revolt with a tiny core of Türk loyalists and within three years held the entire Mongolian steppe up to the Great Wall.

Tonyukuk — Türk-born but raised and educated in Tang China — became chief minister to three successive Second-Khaganate khagans, bringing working knowledge of Tang administrative practice and a deep understanding of how settled empires governed their frontiers.

Tonyukuk argued explicitly against Türk conversion to Buddhism in his autobiographical inscription, claiming that Buddhism made men gentle and weak and that the only sustainable Türk strategy was to stay nomadic, stay in the saddle, and stay out of cities.

Kapaghan Qaghan ruled the Second Khaganate from 691 to 716 at its military peak, defeating the Khitan, raiding deep into Tang territory, and forcing Empress Wu Zetian to absorb a humiliating treaty that recognized Türk parity with the Chinese state.

Kul Tigin secured his brother Bilge's throne in 716 with a night raid on the imperial encampment after Kapaghan was killed by the Bayirku — and Bilge, Kul Tigin, and Tonyukuk became the most stable triumvirate Türk leadership had ever produced.

The three Orkhon Inscriptions — the Tonyukuk inscription of 720, the Kul Tigin inscription of 732, and the Bilge Kagan inscription of 735 — are the oldest surviving texts in any Turkic language, written in a custom Old Turkic script descended from Sogdian.

Bilge's most famous passage, in the Kul Tigin Inscription, warns the Türk people against Chinese silk and gold and sweet words: stay in the Otuken country, send caravans, and you will live forever in your khaganate; go to the southern lands and you will perish.

Bilge was poisoned by his own minister Buyruq Chor in 734, dying slowly enough to have his murderer and the murderer's clan exterminated before he expired; his death deprived the Second Khaganate of its most stable leader and the empire began fracturing within a decade.
subtitle: Ilterish revives the empire, Tonyukuk warns against Sinicization, and Bilge speaks for himself in the Orkhon Inscriptions

title: The Uyghur Take-Over and the Turkic Diaspora
bullets: In 744 a coalition of Uyghurs, Karluks, and Basmyls killed the last Türk khagan Bomei; the Uyghur leader Kutluk Bilge took the imperial title, the Ashina dynasty was extinguished, and the dynasty that had named the Turkic peoples vanished from history.

The Uyghur Khaganate ruled the Mongolian steppe from 744 to 840, converted to Manichaeism in 762 — the only steppe empire to adopt Manichaeism — and built the urban capital of Karabalghasun on the Orkhon, breaking sharply with the Türk tradition of pure nomadism.

The Karluks defected from the Tang to the Abbasid Arab side at the Battle of Talas in 751, sealing the Tang defeat and beginning the Islamic conversion of Central Asia — every later Türk-speaking Muslim state from Karakhanid to Seljuk to Ottoman descended from this religious watershed.

The Khazar Khaganate ruled the lower Volga and the Pontic steppe from roughly 650 to 969, with a Türk-speaking ruling class governing a multi-ethnic population; the royal court converted to Judaism around 740, the only Jewish-ruling state in history outside ancient Israel.

The Bulgars under Asparuh crossed the Danube in 680 and founded the First Bulgarian Empire, ruling a Slavic-speaking population that gradually absorbed the Türk-speaking ruling class — leaving only the country's name as a faint reminder of its Türk political ancestry.

The Seljuks defeated and captured Byzantine Emperor Romanos IV at the Battle of Manzikert in 1071, opening Anatolia to Türk settlement and beginning the conversion of the region from Greek-speaking Christianity to Türk-speaking Islam.

The Ottoman dynasty was founded around 1299 by Osman I from the fragments of the Anatolian Seljuk state and traces continuous Türk identity through the Seljuks to the Oghuz steppe confederations to the Western Türk subject peoples to the Ashina founding in 552.

Two hundred million people across six independent countries call themselves Türks of one variety or another today, and every one of them traces the political ethnonym back to a Mongolian clan that revolted against the Rouran in 552.
subtitle: the Uyghurs replace the Ashina, the Türk peoples scatter across Eurasia, and the name they invented becomes the master ethnonym for two hundred million
